name: Speed boards
blurb: Reflective boards giving the permitted speed — permanent limits and temporary restrictions.
intro: Speed boards tell you the maximum speed for the line ahead. Permanent Speed Restriction (PSR) boards mark a lasting limit; Temporary and Emergency Speed Restriction (TSR/ESR) boards mark a limit imposed for engineering work or a fault, warned in advance and later cancelled. Speeds are in miles per hour.

variants.name: Permissible speed indicator (PSR)
variants.blurb: White reflective board with black figures — the permanent line-speed limit.

aspects.name: Permissible speed
aspects.whatItMeans: The figure is the maximum speed (mph) permitted from this board onwards, until the next speed board. Here, 40 mph.
aspects.whatYouDo: Be at or below the shown speed by the time the front of your train reaches the board.
aspects.lookAlikes: A single white board with black figures is a permanent limit. Two figures stacked is a differential board; a yellow board with two white spots warns of a temporary restriction.
aspects.controls: Brake in good time so you are at the limit by the board.
aspects.realWorldNote: Some PSR boards are preceded by a warning indicator with an AWS magnet where the reduction is large. The game models the boards; the exact warning arrangements vary by route.

variants.name: Differential speed board
variants.blurb: Two figures: a higher limit for some trains, a lower one for others.

aspects.name: Differential speed
aspects.whatItMeans: Two limits apply depending on the type of train. The figure BELOW the line is the higher speed and applies to passenger trains, parcels and light locomotives; the figure ABOVE the line is the lower speed and applies to other traffic such as freight. Here, 90 mph for passenger trains or 60 mph for freight.
aspects.whatYouDo: Obey the figure that applies to the train you are driving. If you are unsure which applies, use the lower (slower) speed.
aspects.controls: Brake to the applicable limit by the board.
aspects.realWorldNote: Which figure applies is set by the train’s classification and route rules. In Train Sim World, follow the limit indicated for your service.

variants.name: TSR warning indicator
variants.blurb: Yellow board with two white spots — warns a temporary restriction is coming.

aspects.name: TSR warning
aspects.meaning: Restriction ahead — be ready to slow
aspects.whatItMeans: A yellow board with two white spots, fitted with an AWS magnet: a temporary speed restriction is coming up, so you get an in-cab warning before the speed indicator.
aspects.whatYouDo: Begin braking so you are at the temporary limit by the commencement (speed) board.
aspects.safetyInteraction: The AWS magnet at the warning board sounds the horn — acknowledge it, then brake for the restriction.
aspects.controls: Acknowledge AWS (default: Q) and brake for the restriction.
aspects.realWorldNote: Exact warning-board distances are set by the engineering instructions for each restriction; this is a representative depiction.

variants.name: TSR speed indicator
variants.blurb: The temporary speed itself — reduce to this figure by the board.

aspects.name: TSR speed
aspects.whatItMeans: The temporary maximum speed (mph) begins here and applies until the termination board. Here, 20 mph.
aspects.whatYouDo: Be at or below this speed by the board, and hold it until the restriction ends.
aspects.controls: Hold the temporary limit until you pass the termination board.

variants.name: TSR termination indicator
variants.blurb: Marks the end of a temporary restriction — resume line speed once clear.

aspects.name: TSR ends
aspects.whatItMeans: The temporary restriction ends here. Once the whole of your train has passed this board, you may return to the permanent line speed.
aspects.whatYouDo: Wait until your entire train is clear of the restriction, then accelerate back to line speed.
aspects.controls: Power up to line speed once the rear of the train has passed.
